skill.name: ckm-design-system
skill.description: Design-system guidance for token architecture, component specifications, Tailwind integration, and brand-compliant slide generation.
skill.summary: Build token-driven design systems and presentation assets with documented CSS variables, component states, and validation scripts.
skill.author: nextlevelbuilder

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer produced many high-severity hits, but most repeated weak-crypto, C2, reconnaissance, and backtick findings are false positives from Markdown, CSV design data, CSS token names, and template literals. No malicious intent or prompt-injection attempt was found, but the skill includes local scripts that can read broad directories and write caller-selected output paths, so it should publish with clear usage warnings.

medium_findings.title: Unconstrained CLI Output Paths
medium_findings.description: The slide and token generators write to caller-provided output paths. This is legitimate local tooling, but unsafe if an AI agent passes untrusted paths or runs in a sensitive workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are direct and depend on CLI-provided output values. The behavior appears intended for local generation, so the risk depends on how arguments are controlled.

medium_findings.title: Broad User-Selected Filesystem Reads
medium_findings.description: The validation tools recursively scan a user-selected directory and read matching source files. This can inspect more project data than intended if invoked with a broad path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts read local files from caller-controlled paths, but the purpose is visible token validation. I did not find evidence that file contents are sent to a remote service.

low_findings.title: Curated External Resource References
low_findings.description: The skill references public Pexels image URLs and generated HTML can load public font resources. These are not credential exfiltration, but users should review external resources before publication.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are public design resources used for slides and fonts. I did not find code that uploads local data to those URLs.

low_findings.title: Subprocess Wrapper Delegates to Local Validator
low_findings.description: The legacy slide validator calls a local Python validator through subprocess.run with an argument list. The pattern is executable behavior, but it does not use a shell string.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is built as an argv array using sys.executable and a local script path. This significantly reduces command injection risk compared with shell execution.

low_findings.title: Static Crypto and C2 Hits Are Design-Token False Positives
low_findings.description: The repeated weak-crypto and C2 findings align with CSS token names, Markdown code fences, radius names such as md, colors, shadows, and design-token examples, not cryptographic code or command-and-control behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections are design-token reference values and JSON token data. I did not find cryptographic functions, malware networking, or C2 logic in those contexts.

dangerous_patterns.title: Caller-Controlled File Output
dangerous_patterns.description: Generation scripts write files to paths derived from CLI arguments. Constrain output paths when using this skill in automated workflows.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write paths are resolved directly from user-provided arguments. The code is local tooling, not confirmed malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Local File Scanning
dangerous_patterns.description: The token validator scans recursively under a caller-provided directory. Run it only against intended project paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The recursive scan is explicit and intended for validation. The concern is over-broad local access if invoked with an unsafe directory.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Public Asset References
dangerous_patterns.description: Generated slide workflows reference public image and font resources. This is normal for presentation assets but should be disclosed to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external references are visible public URLs. I did not find hidden network exfiltration logic.

content.value_statement: Teams need consistent design decisions across components, themes, and presentation assets. This skill provides token architecture, component specifications, validation scripts, and slide-generation guidance.
actual_capabilities: Explains a three-layer token model using primitive, semantic, and component tokens.
Provides reference material for color, spacing, typography, radius, shadow, motion, and component states.
Generates CSS variables or Tailwind color configuration from token JSON through local scripts.
Checks code and HTML assets for hardcoded design values that should use tokens.
Searches CSV slide databases for deck strategy, layout, copy, chart, typography, color, and background recommendations.
Generates HTML slide decks from structured slide data and built-in slide templates.
limitations: The helper scripts require local Node.js or Python runtime support.
Generated slides assume project asset paths such as design-tokens.css exist in the expected location.
The validation scripts report token issues but do not fully refactor application code.
Background image support uses curated public URLs and manual lookup links, not live image licensing checks.

use_cases.title: Create a Token Foundation
use_cases.description: Define primitive, semantic, and component tokens before building a new interface library.

use_cases.title: Validate Frontend Consistency
use_cases.description: Scan UI source files for hardcoded colors, spacing, and font values that bypass design tokens.

use_cases.title: Generate Brand Slides
use_cases.description: Use strategy, layout, chart, copy, and background data to create token-compliant presentation drafts.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Tokens
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current design tokens and explain how to organize them into primitive, semantic, and component layers.

prompt_templates.title: Specify a Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a component specification for a button, including tokens for default, hover, active, disabled, focus, and loading states.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Tailwind Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Map these semantic tokens into a Tailwind theme plan and identify which values should remain CSS variables.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Pitch Deck System
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a token-compliant investor pitch deck with slide strategy, layout choices, typography scale, color treatment, and chart recommendations.

output_examples.input: A team asks how to replace flat color values with reusable tokens.
output_examples.output: A layered token plan that separates raw palette values, semantic roles, and component-specific decisions.

output_examples.input: A developer wants to check whether a React project follows the design system.
output_examples.output: A validation workflow that scans source files, reports hardcoded styling, and recommends token replacements.

output_examples.input: A marketer requests a persuasive product launch deck.
output_examples.output: A slide-by-slide recommendation with narrative arc, layout patterns, chart choices, and brand-safe visual treatments.

best_practices: Start with primitive tokens, then assign semantic meaning before creating component-level tokens.
Keep generated assets under a project-controlled output directory and review files before publishing.
Run validators after major UI or slide changes to catch hardcoded styling drift.
anti_patterns: Do not place raw hex values directly inside reusable components when a token exists.
Do not use the slide generator with untrusted output paths or unreviewed slide data.
Do not treat generated Tailwind or CSS output as final without accessibility and brand review.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build token-based design systems, component specifications, Tailwind mappings, and token-compliant slide assets.

faq.question: Does it include executable scripts?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes local Node.js and Python scripts for token generation, validation, slide search, and slide generation.

faq.question: Can it generate production-ready components?
faq.answer: It provides specifications and token guidance. Implementation quality still depends on the surrounding frontend codebase.

faq.question: Can it create presentation slides?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes CSV-driven slide strategy data and a Python script that generates HTML slide decks.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: The included scripts mainly use local files. Some generated output and helper data reference public fonts, Chart.js, and Pexels URLs.

faq.question: What should users review before running it?
faq.answer: Review script arguments, output paths, generated HTML, and any external resource URLs before using results in a real project.
